formulas.context_text: How do the moments of G depend on the distribution of allelic effects at individual loci? If n loci control the character, our assumption of complete additivity implies $$ G=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(X_{fa,i}+X_{mo,i}\right) $$ (24.16) where $ X_{fa,i}\left(X_{mo,i}\right) $ is the value of the paternal (maternal) allele at the ith locus. Assuming both sexes have the same distribution of allelic effects, the moments of G can be rel

formulas.label: Formula 24.17

Assuming both sexes have the same distribution of allelic effects, the moments of G can be related to moments of the distribution of allelic effects at individual loci by expanding $$ \begin{aligned}\mu_{k,G}&=E\left(\left[G-\mu_{G}\right]^{k}\right)\\&=E\left(\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left\{X_{fa,i}+X_{mo,i}-2E(X_{i})\right\}\right]^{k}\right)\qquad for k\geq2\end{aligned} $$ (24.17)

formulas.subsection: Application: Departure from Normality Under Truncation Selection
formulas.context_text: Bulmer (1980) showed that when the joint distribution of breeding values and phenotypes is multivariate normal before selection, all higher cumulants follow a very simple relationship $$ K_{i,A}^{*}=\left(h^{2}\right)^{i}K_{i,z}^{*}\qquad for\quad i\geq3 $$ (24.24a)

formulas.label: Formula 24.24b
formulas.subsection: Application: Departure from Normality Under Truncation Selection
formulas.context_text: Assuming unlinked loci, the cumulants for the distribution of breeding values in the next generation become $$ K_{i,A}(t+1)=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{i-1}K_{i,A}^{*}(t) $$ (24.24b)

formulas.label: Formula 24.24c
formulas.subsection: Application: Departure from Normality Under Truncation Selection
formulas.context_text: Hence, the cumulants for the distribution of breeding values at the start of the next generation are related to the cumulants of the postselection phenotypic distribution by $$ K_{i,A}(t+1)=2\left(\frac{h^{2}}{2}\right)^{i}K_{i,z}^{*}(t) $$ (24.24c)

formulas.label: Formula 24.25a
